questions.question: Define thread scheduling and explain its importance in modern operating systems.
keyPoints: Thread scheduling is allocating CPU time to competing threads
Enables multi-tasking and resource sharing
Maximizes CPU utilization and system throughput
Essential for responsive interactive and real-time systems

questions.question: Explain the five states in the thread lifecycle with a brief description of each.
keyPoints: NEW: Thread is being created, not yet ready
READY: Thread waiting in queue for CPU allocation
RUNNING: Thread executing on CPU
BLOCKED: Thread waiting for I/O or event
TERMINATED: Thread completed execution

questions.question: Differentiate between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling with an example of each.
keyPoints: Preemptive: OS can forcibly remove thread (Round-Robin)
Non-Preemptive: Thread runs to completion or yields (FCFS)
Preemptive provides better response time
Non-Preemptive simpler to implement

questions.question: What is context switching? Explain why it introduces overhead in thread scheduling.
keyPoints: Context switching saves state of outgoing thread and loads incoming thread state
Includes saving registers, program counter, stack pointer
CPU time spent on switching rather than useful work
Overhead increases with more frequent switching

questions.question: Describe the three multithreading models with one advantage and one disadvantage of each.
keyPoints: Many-to-One: Simple but blocks all threads if one blocks on I/O
One-to-One: True parallelism but limited by kernel thread count
Many-to-Many: Best of both but complex to implement

questions.question: What is thread priority scheduling? Mention one problem associated with it.
keyPoints: Assigns importance levels to threads
Higher priority threads get CPU first
Problem: Starvation of low-priority threads
Solution: Aging - gradually increasing priority of waiting threads

questions.question: What is a thread pool? Explain two benefits of using thread pools in server applications.
keyPoints: Thread pool is pre-created collection of reusable threads
Benefit 1: Reduces overhead of creating new threads for each request
Benefit 2: Limits concurrent threads preventing resource exhaustion
Benefit 3: Improves response time for incoming requests

questions.question: Explain Round-Robin, FCFS, and SJF thread scheduling algorithms with an example showing waiting time calculation for each. Discuss when each algorithm is most suitable.
keyPoints: FCFS: Order of arrival, simple but can cause convoy effect
SJF: Shortest burst first, minimum average waiting but needs knowledge of burst times, may cause starvation
Round-Robin: Time-sliced, fair, suitable for time-sharing systems
Example calculation showing all three methods for same set of thread burst times

questions.question: Explain the five major scheduling criteria used to evaluate thread scheduling algorithms. For a given set of threads with arrival times and burst times, demonstrate how to calculate turnaround time and waiting time.
keyPoints: CPU Utilization, Throughput, Turnaround Time, Waiting Time, Response Time
Turnaround = Completion - Arrival
Waiting = Turnaround - Burst Time
Shows calculation with sample numerical example
Discuss trade-offs between criteria

questions.question: Explain what is meant by thread starvation in priority scheduling. How does aging help solve this problem? Also explain thread affinity and its performance benefits.
keyPoints: Starvation: Low-priority threads may never get CPU if high-priority threads keep arriving
Aging: Gradually increase priority of waiting threads so eventually they get served
Thread affinity: Binding threads to specific CPU cores
Benefits: Better cache performance, predictable behavior

questions.question: Compare one-to-one, many-to-one, and many-to-many threading models. Also explain how thread scheduling differs in user-level threads versus kernel-level threads.
keyPoints: Many-to-One: All user threads on one kernel thread - blocks if any blocks
One-to-One: Each user thread on separate kernel thread - true parallelism
Many-to-Many: Flexible mapping - best of both worlds
User-level scheduling: OS unaware, process decides which thread runs
Kernel-level scheduling: OS scheduler decides, true preemption possible
